confirmed.title: Bespoke modal shells instead of canonical Modal in TaskDetailsModals.jsx
confirmed.detail: Three hand-rolled modal scaffolds (DetailsModal, the image-viewer overlay, DeleteConfirmationModal) each implement their own `fixed inset-0 z-modal/z-top … bg-feedback-scrim` scrim. None import or route through the canonical Modal (src/components/ui/Modal.jsx). DeleteConfirmationModal is also a bespoke confirm-destructive dialog that should be ConfirmDialog. The file has no import of Modal or ConfirmDialog whatsoever.
reasons: Factually the claim checks out — at src/components/TaskDetailsModals.jsx the DetailsModal shell (L20-31), the image-viewer overlay (L469-478), and DeleteConfirmationModal (L578-586) each hand-roll a `fixed inset-0 ... bg-feedback-scrim`/`bg-black/95` scrim, and the file imports neither Modal (src/components/ui/Modal.jsx) nor ConfirmDialog (src/components/ui/ConfirmDialog.jsx). But under the CORRECTNESS lens this is not a real defect, for two reasons. (1) It is not a correctness bug — every shell renders and behaves correctly: all use the shared useModalA11y hook (focus-trap, Escape, focus restore), portal where needed, use token scrim/z classes, and carry proper role/aria-modal/aria-labelledby. This is a design-system *discipline* smell (the prompt itself tags it [discipline/high]), not broken behavior. (2) Chesterton's fence is explicitly documented and already adjudicated against this exact claim. The repo's prior full-sweep audit (docs/audits/full-sweep-2026-06-21/) triaged all three: DetailsModal and the image-viewer were classified 🟡 Risk 'conformance smells' (not bugs), and the DeleteConfirmationModal-duplicates-ConfirmDialog finding was REJECTED as a false positive (1/3 skeptics). Moreover ConfirmDialog's own docstring states it 'Mirrors the good DeleteConfirmationModal' — the bespoke delete dialog is the intentional reference exemplar, kept because it carries a three-way branch (cancel / keep-hours-strike-through-finish / hard-delete) the generic two-button ConfirmDialog cannot express. ADR 0008 also records the image-viewer's bg-black/95 scrim as deliberately outside theme scope ('correct over a photo in any theme'). So: real as a stylistic observation, but not a correctness issue and a deliberate/already-adjudicated design choice — real=false.

Confirmed by reading source. All three scaffolds hand-roll their own overlay and bypass the canonical Modal: DetailsModal (lines 20-31) uses `fixed inset-0 z-modal ... bg-feedback-scrim` with its own dialog card; the image-viewer overlay (lines 469-478) uses `fixed inset-0 z-top ... bg-black/95` via createPortal; DeleteConfirmationModal (lines 579-586) uses `fixed inset-0 z-modal ... bg-feedback-scrim` with bespoke destructive content. A Grep confirms the file imports neither Modal nor ConfirmDialog (only useModalA11y, IconButton, UserChip).

This is NOT a defensible Chesterton's fence. The canonical Modal (src/components/ui/Modal.jsx) is explicitly documented as "the one dialog shell (DESIGN_SYSTEM §8)... so they can never drift apart again," and it supports every case here: `size`/`bare` for DetailsModal and the delete body, and `level="top"` + `bare` + black backdrop for the image viewer. Decisively, ConfirmDialog.jsx's own header says it "Mirrors the good DeleteConfirmationModal" — i.e. the canonical component was modeled ON this file's bespoke dialog, proving the bespoke version is the legacy pattern the design system was built to absorb, not a deliberately preserved special case. There is no in-file comment justifying the divergence. CLAUDE.md mandates "Canonical components only."

One honest caveat (does not change the verdict): DeleteConfirmationModal has a three-way action branch (cancel / keep-hours-and-cross-out / delete-everything) that ConfirmDialog's two-button API cannot express, so a literal swap to ConfirmDialog is imperfect for that one dialog — but the load-bearing claim (the three shells should route their scrim/z-ladder/card through canonical Modal, with custom bodies via `bare` where needed) is fully correct. Severity discipline/high is fair: real design-system drift across three overlays, low ambiguity.

Verified by reading the source. src/components/TaskDetailsModals.jsx hand-rolls three independent modal scaffolds, each re-implementing the scrim/centering/z-ladder that the canonical Modal already owns:

1. DetailsModal (lines 20-31): `<div className="fixed inset-0 z-modal flex items-center justify-center bg-feedback-scrim p-4" ...>` wrapping a `bg-surface-card rounded-modal shadow-xl` card. Re-derives its own dialogRef/useModalA11y/role/aria/titleId.
2. Image-viewer overlay (lines 469-478): `<div className="fixed inset-0 z-top ... bg-black/95 ..." role="dialog" aria-modal>`, manually createPortal'd to body (line 566).
3. DeleteConfirmationModal (lines 579-586): another `fixed inset-0 z-modal ... bg-feedback-scrim p-4` + `bg-surface-card rounded-modal` shell.

The file imports only IconButton from ./ui (line 7); grep confirms NO import of Modal or ConfirmDialog anywhere in the file.

Chesterton's-fence check fails to excuse it: the canonical src/components/ui/Modal.jsx already covers every special need these scaffolds cite — `level="top"` for the image viewer raised above an open task modal, `bare` mode for custom chrome, portal-to-body, the shared bg-feedback-scrim, and the same useModalA11y focus-trap. Modal's own docstring frames it as "the one dialog shell (DESIGN_SYSTEM §8) ... so they can never drift apart again." Decisively, src/components/ui/ConfirmDialog.jsx exists and its docstring says it "Mirrors the good DeleteConfirmationModal" — i.e. the canonical confirm dialog was explicitly modeled on this very bespoke component as the thing to replace, not preserve.

One nuance, not a refutation: DeleteConfirmationModal has a three-way branch (Cancel / keep-work-hours / delete-all via onConfirm({keepWorkHours})) that ConfirmDialog's fixed confirm/cancel API can't express directly — so a literal swap to ConfirmDialog only fits the non-task (two-button) path. But ConfirmDialog itself is just a thin body over Modal, so the task-deletion case should still route its custom three-button body through Modal rather than re-rolling the scrim/portal/a11y by hand. The scaffold-level duplication and the missing canonical-component usage are real and match DESIGN_SYSTEM's "canonical components only" rule (Button/IconButton/Modal/ConfirmDialog; window.confirm banned). discipline/high severity is appropriate.

confirmed.title: TaskModal rolls its own fixed-inset-0 outer shell instead of using Modal
confirmed.detail: The entire TaskModal component is a hand-rolled `fixed inset-0 z-modal flex items-center justify-center bg-feedback-scrim` shell. Despite importing ConfirmDialog for its internal confirm dialogs, the top-level modal frame does not use the canonical Modal component, violating DESIGN_SYSTEM §8 which mandates routing all overlays through Modal.
reasons:
VERIFIED REAL. Read src/components/TaskModal.jsx:695 directly: it returns createPortal(<div className="fixed inset-0 z-modal flex items-center justify-center bg-feedback-scrim p-4">...) — a hand-rolled scrim shell, the exact thing the canonical Modal exists to provide. The import block (lines 1-25) imports Button, IconButton, Select, ConfirmDialog, TaskStatusPill, DeletedBadge and useModalA11y but NOT the canonical Modal (src/components/ui/Modal.jsx), confirming the claim that it imports ConfirmDialog yet bypasses Modal for the top-level frame.

DESIGN_SYSTEM §8 is explicit and binding (lines 216-217): "This is the single rule for all pop-ups; route new overlays through Modal rather than hand-rolling a fixed inset-0 scrim." Modal.jsx's portal (lines 59-68) produces precisely the fixed inset-0 ... bg-feedback-scrim shell TaskModal duplicates.

Chesterton's-fence check fails to find a fence: (1) no DECISION comment anywhere in TaskModal.jsx; (2) no entry in docs/decisions-log.md and no ADR carving out an exception; (3) the canonical Modal already ships a `bare` escape hatch (Modal.jsx lines 99-100) designed for exactly this case — a caller supplying its own full-bleed header/scrollable-body/footer chrome while inheriting the shared scrim, focus-trap and z-ladder — so there was no technical need to fork the shell; (4) all four sibling modals (SessionEditModal, UserProfileModal, BadgeDetailModal, InlineEditModal) import and use the canonical Modal, making TaskModal the sole outlier.

Minor corroborating deviations from the single source of truth: TaskModal places z-modal on the outer scrim and no z-class on the inner card, whereas canonical Modal layers z-backdrop (40) on the scrim and z-modal (50) on the card; it also omits the canonical entry animation (animate-in fade-in zoom-in-95) and re-wires useModalA11y by hand instead of inheriting it. This is a genuine discipline/consistency violation, not a sanctioned design choice.

Verified against source and the design spec. TaskModal.jsx:694-702 hand-rolls its outer dialog shell — createPortal wrapping a `fixed inset-0 z-modal flex items-center justify-center bg-feedback-scrim p-4` div plus an inner `role="dialog" aria-modal` card — instead of routing through the canonical Modal component. It imports ConfirmDialog (line 22) but not Modal; it does reuse useModalA11y (line 25), so the fork duplicates Modal's scrim/centering/card/a11y almost exactly. DESIGN_SYSTEM §8 ("Modal/Dialog") is explicit and binding: "route new overlays through `Modal` rather than hand-rolling a `fixed inset-0` scrim," and states the canonical Modal exists to replace "~10 hand-rolled modal scaffolds." This shell is precisely such a scaffold. Chesterton's-fence check finds no justified design reason for the fork: Modal already ships the exact escape hatches TaskModal's layout needs — `bare` (caller-supplied full-bleed sticky-header / scroll-body / footer chrome, which is TaskModal's structure) and `level` for stacking the internal ConfirmDialogs above it — and TaskModal's `max-w-2xl` maps to Modal's `size="xl"`. There is no capability gap forcing the hand-roll, and no comment/ADR defends it; it reads as un-migrated legacy. Severity nuance: this is a consistency/maintainability (discipline) violation, not a functional bug — the shell still renders and is accessible — but it is a genuine, undefended breach of the §8 single-shell mandate, matching the claim's [discipline/high] framing.

Confirmed by reading source. TaskModal.jsx:694-703 hand-rolls its outer overlay via createPortal(<div className="fixed inset-0 z-modal flex items-center justify-center bg-feedback-scrim p-4">) plus a manually-assembled dialog card (role="dialog", aria-modal, panelRef, tabIndex={-1}, useModalA11y at line 249). It imports only ConfirmDialog (line 22) for internal confirms, NOT the canonical Modal component. DESIGN_SYSTEM.md §8 (lines 216-217) is explicit: "This is the single rule for all pop-ups; route new overlays through Modal rather than hand-rolling a fixed inset-0 scrim," reinforced by the §11 checklist (line 329). Chesterton's-fence check finds no justifying reason: the canonical Modal (src/components/ui/Modal.jsx) already exposes purpose-built escape hatches (bare, level="top") for special cases, and TaskModal needs neither — it is a standard header/scroll-body/footer card that Modal size="xl" (max-w-2xl) renders by default, matching TaskModal's own max-w-2xl. Every comparable overlay routes through Modal (AllUsersCalendar, BadgeDetailModal, CallTimer, QuickWorkTimer, SessionEditModal, NotificationBell), including the genuinely special ones that legitimately use the escape hatches (TaskTimeLimitPopup/TaskTimeWarningPopup with bare+level="top", DailyStatistics with bare). TaskModal is the sole outlier, with no DECISION marker or comment justifying the fork, and it duplicates the very useModalA11y hook Modal calls internally — a sign of parallel reimplementation, not a deliberate exception. Real §8 discipline violation. (Caveat: it reads as un-migrated legacy rather than a fresh regression, but the claim is on the conformance axis, where it holds.)

confirmed.title: ActiveSessionReadout duplicates session palette in a local READOUT map instead of reading SESSION_COLORS
confirmed.detail: The component defines its own local `READOUT` object with hardcoded session token class strings for quickWork/call/break, duplicating exactly what SESSION_COLORS provides. It does not import SESSION_COLORS at all. DESIGN_SYSTEM §4-B states 'the shell background, the timer pill, and the running task card all read the same SESSION_COLORS token map — they must never drift.'
reasons:
VERIFIED REAL. Read src/components/ActiveSessionReadout.jsx:11-30 and src/utils/sessionColors.js directly. The component defines a local `READOUT` map for quickWork/call/break that hardcodes (a) labels ('Greitas darbas','Skambutis','Pertrauka'), (b) icons (Zap/Phone/Coffee), and (c) color token classes inside `tone` (bg-session-<type>-surface, text-session-<type>-accent, plus border-session-<type>-accent). It never imports SESSION_COLORS. SESSION_COLORS in src/utils/sessionColors.js already provides exactly these per type: `label`, `Icon`, `surface`, `accent`. So label/icon/surface/accent are pure duplication of the single-source-of-truth map. DESIGN_SYSTEM.md §4-B (lines 91-93) is binding: "The shell background, the timer pill, and the running task card all read the same SESSION_COLORS token map. They must never drift." This readout IS the timer pill, so this is the precise failure §4-B forbids — if a palette/label/icon ever changes in the map, this pill silently keeps the stale value.

Chesterton's-fence check: the local map does carry two fields not in SESSION_COLORS — `stateKey` (quickWorkState/callState/breakState) and a `border-session-*-accent` border class. That legitimately requires SOME local data, but it does NOT justify re-hardcoding the surface, accent, label, and icon, which the map already owns; those should be read from SESSION_COLORS with only stateKey + border composed locally. No comment or ADR claims the duplication is intentional (the surrounding comments only explain pill layout and the a11y live region). The sibling component ActiveWorkSessions.jsx:82 follows the correct pattern via getSessionColors('quickWork'), confirming read-from-map is the established convention and this component is an outlier, not a sanctioned exception. Real discipline/drift issue.

Relevant files: C:\Users\karol\Desktop\WORKZ\.claude\worktrees\stoic-proskuriakova-aad0bf\src\components\ActiveSessionReadout.jsx (lines 11-30), C:\Users\karol\Desktop\WORKZ\.claude\worktrees\stoic-proskuriakova-aad0bf\src\utils\sessionColors.js, C:\Users\karol\Desktop\WORKZ\.claude\worktrees\stoic-proskuriakova-aad0bf\docs\design\DESIGN_SYSTEM.md (§4-B, lines 91-93).

Confirmed real after reading source. src/components/ActiveSessionReadout.jsx (lines 11-30) defines a local READOUT map that hardcodes session presentation for quickWork/call/break: tone strings 'border-session-X-accent bg-session-X-surface text-session-X-accent', plus the labels ('Greitas darbas'/'Skambutis'/'Pertrauka') and icons (Zap/Phone/Coffee). The file does NOT import SESSION_COLORS (imports at lines 1-6 confirm this). src/utils/sessionColors.js is the documented single source of truth (DESIGN_SYSTEM §4-B) and already exposes exactly these per-type values: label, Icon, accent (text-session-X-accent), surface (bg-session-X-surface). So the readout pill — a session-state presentation surface, precisely the kind §4-B says must read the shared map so it 'never drifts' — restates all of it verbatim, including the same color/label/icon data. Chesterton's-fence check fails to exonerate it: the local map's only genuinely-extra fields are stateKey (legacy lastStartedAt lookup) and a pre-composed border+bg+text 'tone' string. SESSION_COLORS lacks a combined border-token triad, but it does provide the accent text color and surface bg that compose it, plus label and Icon — so the component could keep a tiny local stateKey map (and derive border from accent) while sourcing label/icon/colors from SESSION_COLORS. No comment justifies the copy, and sessionColors.js's own doc explicitly warns against this drift. This is genuine token/label/icon duplication of the canonical palette, not a deliberate exception. (Severity is discipline/maintainability, not a runtime bug — values happen to match today.)

CONFIRMED REAL via reproducibility lens. Read src/components/ActiveSessionReadout.jsx:11-30: it defines a local `READOUT` map with hardcoded session token classes (`bg-session-quickWork-surface text-session-quickWork-accent`, and call/break equivalents) plus re-declared labels ('Greitas darbas', 'Skambutis', 'Pertrauka') and re-imported icons (Zap/Phone/Coffee at line 2). ActiveSessionReadout IS the floating timer pill, so it is squarely in §4-B scope. Chesterton's-fence check fails to exonerate it: the established pattern in sibling consumers is to read the map — Layout.jsx (getSessionColors → shell/onShell) and SessionTypeIcon.jsx (session.accent + session.Icon) both do so. The only token READOUT adds that the map lacks is `border-session-*-accent` (no `border` field exists in SESSION_COLORS), and `stateKey` is genuinely local non-presentational data — but a missing one-line border field and a fallback key do not justify a fully parallel palette; the correct fix is to derive surface/accent/label/icon from getSessionColors and optionally extend the map with a border token. confirmed.title: archiveOldTasks: UTC date extracted from ISO timestamp compared against Vilnius date string
confirmed.detail: Line 153 extracts the calendar date from a stored ISO timestamp with `relevantDate.split('T')[0]`, which yields the UTC date (e.g. '2025-06-22' for '2025-06-22T22:30:00Z'). Line 154 compares this against `cutOffStr` which is a Vilnius local date string produced by `getLithuanianDateString`. In summer (UTC+3) any task confirmed between 21:00 and 00:00 Vilnius time will have a UTC date one day earlier than the corresponding Vilnius date; the UTC date string sorts BEFORE `cutOffStr`, so the task is archived on the WRONG day (one day too early). A task confirmed at 23:00 Vilnius on day D will be archived during the automation run on day D rather than day D+1. The fix is to bucket `relevantDate` through `getLithuanianDateString(new Date(relevantDate))` before the string comparison, identical to what `checkAndPromoteTasks` already does at line 40 for deadlines.
reasons:
Confirmed real after reading src/utils/automationUtils.js and src/utils/timeUtils.js.

Line 153 `const dateStr = relevantDate.split('T')[0];` extracts the UTC calendar date from a stored ISO timestamp (confirmedAt/deletedAt/updatedAt are persisted as UTC ISO strings — see line 61 `new Date().toISOString()`). Line 154 then compares this UTC date string against `cutOffStr`, which is built from `getLithuanianDateString(now)` (lines 142-145) — a Vilnius-local date string. Comparing a UTC-derived date against a Vilnius-derived date is the defect.

The divergence is genuine: in summer (UTC+3) an instant like '2025-06-22T22:30:00Z' is 2025-06-23 01:30 in Vilnius, so its true Vilnius work-day is 06-23 but `.split('T')[0]` yields '2025-06-22'. The UTC date sorts BEFORE the Vilnius cutoff, so the task is archived one cycle too early. (The claim's example timestamp is internally consistent and the bug direction is correct; its prose '21:00–00:00 Vilnius' is slightly imprecise — the actual divergence window is Vilnius 00:00–03:00 / UTC previous-day 21:00–24:00 — but this does not affect the validity of the finding.)

Not a Chesterton's-fence design choice: the sibling function `checkAndPromoteTasks` in the SAME file deliberately buckets its date through `getLithuanianDateString(new Date(task.deadline))` at line 40, with an explicit comment (lines 24-28) explaining that raw UTC-string comparison buckets on the wrong day and fires a day off. `archiveOldTasks` simply fails to apply that same, already-established correct pattern — an inconsistency/oversight. The proposed fix (bucket `relevantDate` via `getLithuanianDateString(new Date(relevantDate))` before the comparison) is exactly right.

VERIFIED REAL. At src/utils/automationUtils.js:153-154, `archiveOldTasks` does `const dateStr = relevantDate.split('T')[0]` then `if (dateStr < cutOffStr)`. `relevantDate` is `task.deletedAt || task.confirmedAt || task.updatedAt`, all of which are stored as UTC ISO strings (e.g. confirmedAt/updatedAt come from `new Date().toISOString()` in taskActions.js and line 61 here), so `.split('T')[0]` yields the UTC calendar date. `cutOffStr` is derived from `getLithuanianDateString(now)` (timeZone Europe/Vilnius) and is therefore a Vilnius calendar-date string. Comparing a UTC date string against a Vilnius date string buckets summer (UTC+3) just-past-midnight events on the wrong day.

Reproduced concretely with the real helper code: a task confirmed at Vilnius 2025-06-23 01:30 stores `2025-06-22T22:30:00Z`; `.split('T')[0]` = `2025-06-22`, but its Vilnius date is `2025-06-23`. When the once-per-day automation runs later that same Vilnius day (e.g. 2025-06-23 10:00 Vilnius), `cutOffStr` = `2025-06-23`, so `'2025-06-22' < '2025-06-23'` is true and the task is ARCHIVED a day too early. Bucketing `relevantDate` through `getLithuanianDateString(new Date(relevantDate))` first makes the decision `false` (correctly not archived) — exactly the claimed fix.

Chesterton's-fence check fails to exonerate it: this is an oversight, not a deliberate choice. The sibling function `checkAndPromoteTasks` in the SAME file (line 40, with an explicit comment at lines 24-28) already buckets its date through `getLithuanianDateString(new Date(task.deadline))` precisely to avoid this UTC-vs-Vilnius mismatch, and `archiveOldTasks` itself computes `cutOffStr` carefully in Vilnius time (lines 136-145) — then line 153 silently undoes that care by comparing against a raw UTC split. The codebase's own established pattern IS the proposed fix.

Severity timetracking/high is justified: premature archival removes confirmed/deleted tasks from the active 'tasks' collection a day early, affecting field workers in the LT summer.

Caveat (does not change the verdict): the claim's narrative that the trigger is tasks 'confirmed between 21:00 and 00:00 Vilnius' is imprecise — the actual divergence window is Vilnius 00:00–02:59 (UTC time-of-day 21:00–23:59 the prior UTC day). The mechanism, file/line citation, severity, and recommended fix are all otherwise accurate.

CONFIRMED REAL (with one inaccurate detail in the claim's framing). At src/utils/automationUtils.js:150-154, `relevantDate` is task.deletedAt/confirmedAt/updatedAt — all stored as `new Date().toISOString()` (verified: sessionActions.js:480, taskCompletionActions.js:49/82, sessionEditActions.js:102, taskActions.js, etc.), i.e. UTC ISO strings with a 'Z'. Line 153 `relevantDate.split('T')[0]` therefore yields the UTC calendar date. confirmed.title: calendarNotifications.js weekId computed in browser-local time, not Europe/Vilnius
confirmed.detail: `startOfWeek(now, { weekStartsOn: 1 })` and `format(weekStart, 'yyyy-MM-dd')` come from date-fns and operate entirely in the browser's local timezone, not Europe/Vilnius. When a worker on a non-Vilnius device (e.g. UTC+0 in winter, or a device with an incorrect clock) logs a calendar change on a Sunday evening Vilnius time that is still Sunday UTC, `weekStart` resolves to the correct Monday. But on a UTC+0 browser at e.g. 23:30 Monday Vilnius (22:30 UTC Monday), `startOfWeek` correctly gives Monday. The real risk is the reverse: a UTC-offset browser where the local clock is one day ahead — the weekId written by `logCalendarChange` (worker side, line 12) will disagree with the weekId used to QUERY notifications in `ManagerNotifications.jsx` line 54 (manager side), because both independently call `startOfWeek(new Date(), ...)` and each will resolve to a different YYYY-MM-DD string when the browsers straddle a week boundary in their local clocks. This creates silent notification loss: a calendar change logged at 23:50 Sunday local-time on a UTC+2 browser becomes week W, while a manager viewing on a UTC+0 browser at the same wall-clock moment computes week W-1 and the notification document never appears. The shared weekId should be computed with `getLithuanianDateString` + Monday-of-week arithmetic (as the rest of the codebase does) to guarantee both sides agree.
reasons: Verified in source on BOTH sides. Writer src/utils/calendarNotifications.js:8-12: `now = getLithuanianNow()` which is literally `new Date()` (timeUtils.js:180-182, NO timezone applied), then `weekStart = startOfWeek(now,{weekStartsOn:1})` and `weekId = format(weekStart,'yyyy-MM-dd')` — both date-fns, browser-local time. Reader src/components/ManagerNotifications.jsx:52-58: identical `new Date()` → `startOfWeek` → `format(...,'yyyy-MM-dd')`, used to `query(... where('weekStart','==', weekId))`. The shared cross-device document key `${uid}_${weekId}` is thus derived independently from each device's LOCAL clock, not Europe/Vilnius. Near the Monday week boundary, two devices in different timezones (or a device with a skewed clock) resolve different YYYY-MM-DD strings: a change logged on a UTC+3 worker device just after local Monday midnight is keyed week W, while a UTC+0 manager at the same instant queries week W-1 → the notification document never matches → SILENT loss, with no fallback (the listener queries only the single current weekId, no adjacent-week catch-up). Chesterton's-fence check cuts AGAINST it being intentional: the codebase's own convention for persisted/shared week strings is to funnel startOfWeek through getLithuanianDateString (Intl Europe/Vilnius) — done in DailyHoursSummary.jsx:78-82 and CombinedHoursSummary.jsx:38-40 — so these two files violate the established Vilnius-normalization convention rather than following a deliberate local-time design. The codebase elsewhere explicitly treats non-Vilnius/skewed clocks as in-scope (clampSessionMinutes, DST-safe cutoffs), so the prior audit's 1/3 "not reachable" rejection understates it. Severity is bounded (narrow window near Sunday→Monday midnight, requires timezone divergence) but the failure mode is genuine silent notification loss, and the claimed fix (use getLithuanianDateString on both sides) is correct and matches the existing pattern. The claim's mechanism is accurate; only a couple of its illustrative examples are loosely worded, which does not affect the core defect.

VERIFIED REAL. Read src/utils/calendarNotifications.js:8-13 and src/components/ManagerNotifications.jsx:52-58. Both sides independently bucket by the same recipe: weekStart = startOfWeek(now, {weekStartsOn:1}); weekId = format(weekStart, 'yyyy-MM-dd'). date-fns startOfWeek and format both operate in the runtime's LOCAL timezone, and getLithuanianNow() (timeUtils.js:180) is just `new Date()` with no Vilnius conversion — so the week bucket is computed in each device's local time, not Europe/Vilnius. The writer (a worker) sets docId = uid_weekId and field weekStart=weekId; the reader (a manager, different device) queries where('weekStart','==',weekId) — an EXACT string match with no tolerance. When two devices straddle the Sun-night/Mon boundary in their local clocks (worker on a non-Vilnius tz, or a device with a wrong clock), the two YYYY-MM-DD strings differ by one week and the document silently never matches → notification loss. Chesterton's-fence check FAILS to find an intentional reason: the codebase's OWN convention for deriving a week-start STRING routes startOfWeek through the Vilnius-tz formatter getLithuanianDateString (CombinedHoursSummary.jsx:38-40, DailyHoursSummary.jsx:78-82); these two notification call sites use raw `format` instead, deviating from that pattern. The author even reached for getLithuanianNow() signalling Vilnius intent, but that helper is a no-op `new Date()`, so the intent isn't realized — an oversight, not a design choice. The proposed fix (use getLithuanianDateString / Vilnius-anchored Monday arithmetic on both sides) matches the established convention. Caveat on magnitude only: the failure window is narrow (near the week boundary, and requires the two devices to disagree on the bucket), so 'medium' severity is fair — silent and consequential but not constant. The core defect is genuine and not a deliberate design choice.

Verified by reading src/utils/calendarNotifications.js:8-12, src/utils/timeUtils.js:180-195, and src/components/ManagerNotifications.jsx:52-54. The mechanical premise is accurate: getLithuanianNow() (timeUtils.js:181) is an identity wrapper returning plain `new Date()` with NO Vilnius conversion, so startOfWeek(now,{weekStartsOn:1}) + format(...,'yyyy-MM-dd') do run in browser-local time, not Europe/Vilnius. However, the claim does not clear the skeptic bar, for three reasons. (1) Symmetry by construction, not a bug: the writer (calendarNotifications.js:11-12) and the reader/query (ManagerNotifications.jsx:53-54) use the IDENTICAL local-time computation, so two devices on the same timezone always produce the same weekId and the feature works. WORKZ is a single-country (Lithuania) work-time tracker — both worker and manager run on Europe/Vilnius clocks in real deployment, so they agree. (2) The proposed fix's justification is factually wrong: the claim says the shared weekId 'should be computed with getLithuanianDateString + Monday arithmetic (as the rest of the codebase does)', but the rest of the codebase does NOT do this — WorkPlanner.jsx:385 uses the same startOfWeek(now,{weekStartsOn:1}) local pattern. There is no getLithuanianDateString-based weekId convention to align to; the symmetric local approach IS the convention (Chesterton's fence intact). (3) The failure scenario requires worker and manager browsers in DIFFERENT timezones straddling a Monday boundary at the same UTC instant, which the claim itself qualifies as a 'non-Vilnius device' or 'incorrect clock' edge case — outside this app's normal usage; and a wrong device clock degrades far more than this one feature. The claim's own worked example is internally muddled (it conflates 'same wall-clock moment' with 'same UTC instant' in one sentence, and the UTC+2/UTC+0 walkthrough partly contradicts itself). Net: a defensible intentional design for a same-timezone product, with a fix rationale that misdescribes the codebase — not a clear real defect.

confirmed.detail: Same root cause as the calendarNotifications.js finding: `startOfWeek(new Date(), { weekStartsOn: 1 })` at line 53 and `format(weekStart, 'yyyy-MM-dd')` at line 54 are browser-local. This is exactly the "they must never drift" condition DESIGN_SYSTEM.md §4 Rule B forbids ("The shell background, the timer pill, and the running task card all read the same SESSION_COLORS token map... that is a bug to fix"). tokens.md:73 reinforces: "All session-aware code reads this one map."
3. The hardcoded value would not track a token revision — correct.

Chesterton's-fence check passed (no defensible intent): the tokens.md contrast note (lines 85-87) says the accent was DARKENED to red-700/#B91C1C specifically to clear >=4.5:1 against the surface tint. red-900/#7F1D1D is darker still, so it does NOT break AA contrast — there is no accessibility rationale for deviating from the token. No comment, DECISION marker, or design note justifies the hardcode, and the very same JSX uses the token correctly on adjacent lines, showing it was available and simply applied inconsistently.

Caveat on severity (matches the medium claim, not high): the user-visible difference between #7F1D1D and #B91C1C is a subtle shade on a light pink surface, and red-200 border/ring are close to a token-derived tint, so there is no functional/contrast regression. The defect is genuine token-drift / Rule-B inconsistency, not a visual or accessibility break. The claim's color facts and the cited token (#B91C1C as quickWork accent) are all accurate.

confirmed.title: Rule B drift — BreakTimer desktop button uses hardcoded amber values instead of session tokens
confirmed.detail: The desktop (non-compact) active state of the BreakTimer button is styled with `bg-session-break-surface text-amber-800 hover:bg-amber-100 border border-amber-200`. The text, hover, and border values are raw Tailwind amber constants rather than session tokens. According to DESIGN_SYSTEM §4-B all presentation must come from the SESSION_COLORS map. The correct equivalents are: `text-session-break-accent` (accent, #B45309), `hover:bg-session-break-shell` or `hover:bg-session-break-surface` (hover tint), and `border-session-break-shell` or the session surface token for the border. If the break token palette is updated, this button will not follow.
reasons:
VERIFIED REAL. src/components/BreakTimer.jsx:157 — the active (non-compact) break-button state is styled `'bg-session-break-surface text-amber-800 hover:bg-amber-100 border border-amber-200'`. Three of four color classes (text-amber-800, hover:bg-amber-100, border-amber-200) are raw Tailwind amber constants; only the base bg is token-sourced. This bypasses the SESSION_COLORS map (src/utils/sessionColors.js) that is meant to be the single source of truth for session presentation, violating DESIGN_SYSTEM principle 4 ("Tokens, not magic numbers ... a raw hex/arbitrary value in a component is a bug") and §4 Rule B ("One source of truth ... must never drift"). The consequence stated is accurate: tailwind.config.js defines break tokens only as shell=#FEF3C7, surface=#FFFBEB, accent=#B45309, so if the palette is re-tinted these raw amber literals will not follow.

Chesterton's-fence check passed — NOT a deliberate choice: no ADR, decisions-log entry, inline DECISION comment, or git-history note justifies raw amber here. The same file tokenizes everywhere else (line 112 base bg, line 141 timer text) and the sibling ActiveSessionReadout.jsx:28 fully tokenizes the identical break state, so line 157 is inconsistent drift, not an established exception. A stray `ring-amber-100` on the compact variant (line 112) corroborates incidental drift.

Two precision caveats on the proposed FIX (do not affect validity): (1) text-amber-800 is #92400E, but the suggested text-session-break-accent is #B45309 (amber-700) — lighter, so it is a small behavior change, not a neutral swap (the claim mislabels amber-800 as '#B45309'). (2) border-amber-200 (#FDE68A) has NO exact token equivalent; border-session-break-shell (#FEF3C7) is only an approximation. hover:bg-amber-100 does map exactly to session-break-shell. Severity medium (maintainability/token-drift, not a functional or a11y defect) is appropriate.

CONFIRMED REAL. Read src/components/BreakTimer.jsx:157 — the desktop (non-compact) active-break button's className is exactly `'bg-session-break-surface text-amber-800 hover:bg-amber-100 border border-amber-200'`. The background was migrated to the session token (`bg-session-break-surface`) but the text/hover/border are still raw Tailwind amber constants. This is a genuine Rule B drift, not a design choice.

Evidence the tokens exist and are the canonical path:
- tailwind.config.js (lines 47-52) defines `session.break.{shell:#FEF3C7, surface:#FFFBEB, accent:#B45309}`, so `text-session-break-accent`, `hover:bg-session-break-shell`, and `border-session-break-shell` are all valid token classes (the claim's proposed fixes are correct).
- src/utils/sessionColors.js is the SESSION_COLORS single-source map and exposes break as `accent: 'text-session-break-accent'`, `shell: 'bg-session-break-shell'`, `surface: 'bg-session-break-surface'`.

Rule is binding and correctly cited: DESIGN_SYSTEM.md §4-B (lines 91-93) "One source of truth ... read the same SESSION_COLORS token map. They must never drift"; §3 (lines 48-49) "A raw hex or an arbitrary text-[9px] in a component is a bug"; CLAUDE.md repeats "source it from one SESSION_COLORS map" and "Tokens, not magic numbers." The active-break button renders the break SESSION state, so it falls squarely under Rule B (unlike the amber in ManagerNotifications.jsx / TaskTimeWarningPopup.jsx, which are warning/feedback surfaces, not session presentation, and are out of scope).

Chesterton's fence check — no intentional reason found: there is no comment, decisions-log entry, or ADR justifying a bespoke amber here. The drift is corroborated by the file's own compact branch (line 112), which uses the session token `bg-session-break-accent text-white` for the same active state, and by the sibling ActiveSessionReadout.jsx, which also consumes session-break tokens. This reads as an incomplete token migration (bg- moved to token, text/hover/border left behind).

Note (not a defeater, actually reinforces the claim): the raw values aren't even pixel-identical to the tokens — text-amber-800=#92400E vs accent #B45309; border-amber-200=#FDE68A vs shell #FEF3C7 — i.e. the button was hand-tuned off-palette, which is precisely the drift Rule B prevents. Severity matches the medium label: cosmetic/maintainability (the button won't follow if the break palette is retuned), no functional or a11y impact.

VERIFIED REAL. Read src/components/BreakTimer.jsx:157 directly. The desktop (non-compact) active state literally reads: `'bg-session-break-surface text-amber-800 hover:bg-amber-100 border border-amber-200'`. The premises hold: `text-amber-800`, `hover:bg-amber-100`, and `border-amber-200` are raw Tailwind amber constants, not session tokens. The break token map exists (tokens.md / tailwind.config.js: session.break = shell #FEF3C7 / surface #FFFBEB / accent #B45309), and DESIGN_SYSTEM §4 explicitly frames hardcoded values as "legacy to be migrated, not precedent," with §4-B requiring session-aware presentation to read the one SESSION_COLORS map. So this is genuine token debt, and it is NOT a deliberate design choice — there is no Chesterton's-fence reason to bypass tokens here.

Two important caveats that temper severity (low/medium, not a unique regression):
1) This is a SYSTEMIC pattern, not BreakTimer-specific "drift." CallTimer.jsx:39 uses `border-blue-200`; QuickWorkTimer.jsx:323 uses `border-red-200 text-red-900 ring-red-200`; BreakTimer's own compact state (line 112) uses `ring-amber-100`. Every session timer pairs a session-token background with raw same-hue palette accents/borders/rings. So this is a uniform unmigrated gap across the timer family, and the hue is correct amber — unlike the §4-B canonical "drift" example (blue-vs-sky, a wrong-hue bug). The button would still render amber if the palette changed; it just wouldn't auto-track.
2) The claim's prescribed fix is partly INACCURATE. It says the border should be `border-session-break-shell` (amber-100 #FEF3C7) — but the surface is amber-50 (#FFFBEB), so an amber-100 border would be nearly invisible; the code deliberately uses amber-200 (#FDE68A, one step darker) for a visible edge, and no token for that shade exists in the map. Likewise `text-amber-800` (#92400E) is darker than the break accent token amber-700 (#B45309), so swapping is a real color change for contrast, not a no-op rename.

Bottom line: the hardcoded-vs-token assertion is factually true and present in source; the issue is real consistent token debt, though the proposed token substitutions are imperfect and severity is "migration debt" rather than a regression.

confirmed.title: Worker can self-forge a confirmed/approved task on CREATE by calling the Firestore API directly
confirmed.detail: The `changesApprovalFields()` guard that prevents workers from flipping `status` to `'confirmed'`/`'approved'`, `isApproved`, `confirmedBy`, or `approvedBy` is applied only on UPDATE (line 208-214). The CREATE rule has no equivalent check. The rule comment at lines 129-133 acknowledges this intentionally to allow call/quick-work auto-logging. However, the consequence is that any active worker can bypass the entire approval workflow by sending a raw `addDoc` to the `tasks` collection with `{ status: 'confirmed', isApproved: true, confirmedBy: '<uid>', assignedUserId: '<own uid>' }`. The client (`TaskModal.jsx` lines 571-592, `taskCompletionActions.js` lines 29-57) correctly gates the `'confirmed'` status behind a client-side `isManagerRole()` check, but Firestore rules never enforce this on CREATE. The firestore.rules comment explicitly calls out that create is intentionally not guarded, accepting the risk; this finding surfaces it as an unmitigated path for any active worker.
reasons:
VERIFIED REAL (integrity/correctness gap, medium is defensible). The mechanism is exactly as claimed. firestore.rules:200-204 (CREATE on /tasks) guards only assignee-ownership/team-scope; it never calls changesApprovalFields(). The UPDATE rule (208-214) DOES enforce `!changesApprovalFields()` on the owner branch (line 213), so the asymmetry is real: a worker can do via a raw addDoc what they're blocked from doing via updateDoc. A direct-API call with {assignedUserId:<own uid>, status:'confirmed', isApproved:true, confirmedBy:<own uid>, approvedBy:<own uid>} is accepted by the rules — the assignedUserId==auth.uid branch (line 201) passes and nothing checks the approval fields on create. The client (TaskModal.jsx ~579-587 sets workers to status:'unapproved' and fires a manager approval notification; taskCompletionActions.js:47 gates 'confirmed' behind isManagerRole) enforces the workflow only client-side; Firestore does not re-enforce it on CREATE. Net effect: a worker bypasses the approval queue and self-attests their own task as manager-confirmed/approved, corrupting any consumer that treats status=='confirmed'/isApproved as a manager sign-off.

Chesterton's-fence check (rules:127-133): the comment knowingly leaves CREATE unguarded so the legitimate call/quick-work auto-log path (own task born 'confirmed') keeps working. That justification covers the auto-confirm case but does NOT justify leaving isApproved/approvedBy/confirmedBy of an arbitrary manually-created task forgeable — so it is an acknowledged-but-unmitigated gap, not a fully-defended design choice.

Severity caveat (the claim overstates one angle): the obvious high-value escalation — farming the recognition 'approved_craft' badge — does NOT work via this vector. functions/index.js onTaskFinishedBadge (line 413) is an onDocumentUpdated trigger, so it never fires on a forged CREATE, and even on UPDATE it is guarded by `after.confirmedBy !== uid` (line 432), so self-confirmation grants no badge. Impact is also bounded to the attacker's OWN self-assigned records (the create branch binds assignedUserId to auth.uid; team/overseer branches require real scope) — no cross-user tampering. So the integrity bug is real and worth fixing (add a create-time guard, e.g. allow self-confirmed status only for the call/quick-work types and force isApproved/approvedBy to manager-set values), but its blast radius is the attacker's own approval state, which keeps it at medium, not higher.

VERIFIED REAL. I read firestore.rules:200-204 (tasks CREATE) and 208-214 (UPDATE), the changesApprovalFields() helper (134-140), and the client sites. Every factual premise holds: (1) The tasks CREATE rule (200-204) lets a worker create any task where assignedUserId == request.auth.uid, with NO check on status/isApproved/confirmedBy/approvedBy. (2) changesApprovalFields() compares request.resource.data vs resource.data, so it is structurally UPDATE-only and is wired only into line 213 (ownsAssignedUser() && !changesApprovalFields()); CREATE has no equivalent. (3) The comment at 127-133 explicitly admits this is intentional to allow call/quick-work auto-logging. (4) The Chesterton's-fence reason is genuine, not hypothetical: sessionActions.js:467-483 creates a self-assigned task with status:"confirmed", confirmedBy:userId, completed:true via raw addDoc when a worker ends a call. (5) Client gating (TaskModal.jsx:580 forces 'unapproved' for non-managers; taskCompletionActions.js:47-48 gates 'confirmed'/confirmedBy behind isManagerRole()) is client-side only and not mirrored in rules on CREATE. CONCLUSION: this is a real, under-mitigated security gap, not a clean design choice. A worker can bypass the approval workflow by sending a raw addDoc with {status:'confirmed', isApproved:true, confirmedBy:'<own uid>', assignedUserId:'<own uid>'}, fabricating manager-confirmed/approved tasks that feed Reports, statistics and badge eligibility. The documented rationale only requires permitting self-confirmed CREATE for the auto-log path (which already sets isSystemTask:true) — it does NOT require leaving ARBITRARY self-confirmed creates open. So a narrow compensating control is available (e.g. allow self-set confirmed/approved on CREATE only when isSystemTask==true) and is absent; the fence has a reason but is wider than its reason needs, leaving a genuine privilege-escalation path. Severity medium is appropriate (integrity/workflow-bypass, not data exfiltration; requires an authenticated active worker crafting a direct API call).

Confirmed by reading firestore.rules:200-204 (CREATE) against 134-140 (changesApprovalFields) and 208-214 (UPDATE). The CREATE rule for /tasks only checks ownership of assignedUserId / team scope; it has NO equivalent of the changesApprovalFields() guard that UPDATE uses. So a worker calling addDoc directly with {assignedUserId:<own uid>, status:'confirmed', isApproved:true, confirmedBy:<own uid>, approvedBy:<own uid>} passes the rule and creates a self-owned task already marked confirmed AND approved. The client (taskCompletionActions.js:47-48 gates 'confirmed'/confirmedBy behind isManagerRole; TaskCard.jsx:85-87 gates approvedBy behind manager) enforces this, but rules do not — a genuine client-vs-server enforcement gap. The forged state is trusted downstream: Reports.jsx:1051 renders item.approvedBy as the approving manager's name, so a self-approved task surfaces as legitimately manager-approved work. Chesterton's-fence check: the comment at 127-133 does acknowledge CREATE is intentionally unguarded, but its justification only covers the call/quick-work self-CONFIRMED flow and frames the residual risk as merely 'flipping an EXISTING task.' That rationale does not justify self-APPROVAL (isApproved/approvedBy is a higher manager-only gate than confirmation) and understates the blast radius — a worker can manufacture a brand-new already-approved task that reporting treats as genuine sign-off. So this is an acknowledged-but-under-mitigated gap, not a fully-justified design choice. Severity is reasonably medium: limited to self-assigned rows (cannot forge for others, cannot flip a manager-assigned task), but it does defeat the confirm/approve audit trail in reports. Real.

confirmed.title: work_hours UPDATE does not pin the userId field — owner can re-assign a record to another user
confirmed.detail: `allow update, delete: if isUserActive() && (ownsUserId() || isManagerOrAdmin())` — `ownsUserId()` checks that `resource.data.userId == request.auth.uid` (the EXISTING stored value), but the rule does not assert `request.resource.data.userId == resource.data.userId`. A worker who owns a `work_hours` document can therefore submit an update that changes the `userId` field to a different user's uid, effectively re-stamping the record as belonging to a colleague. After the write the record is no longer owned by the original worker, so subsequent `ownsUserId()` checks will pass for the victim's uid instead. This allows a worker to either orphan their own records (so they are no longer deleteable by themselves) or pollute a colleague's planner data without manager privilege. The same gap exists for `work_sessions` and `break_sessions` updates.
reasons:
CONFIRMED REAL via source at firestore.rules:270 (and :238-242 work_sessions, :251-255 break_sessions). The UPDATE rules gate on `ownsUserId() || isManagerOrAdmin()` (or scoped-overseer variant). `ownsUserId()` (lines 117-119) = `resource.data.userId == request.auth.uid` — it checks the EXISTING stored value. None of the three UPDATE rules assert `request.resource.data.userId == resource.data.userId`, so the incoming write may carry a different userId. Firestore evaluates the rule once against the pre-update doc, so an owning worker passes `ownsUserId()` and can re-stamp `userId` to a colleague's uid in the same write. The claim's mechanism is accurate for all three collections.

This is exploitable, not a Chesterton's-fence design choice. The in-file comment (lines 105-115) and the project memory state the explicit invariant: WRITE is owner-scoped precisely to stop tampering — "a worker can see a colleague's hours but cannot mutate them." The userId-reassignment gap defeats exactly that guarantee without manager privilege: a worker can (a) pollute a colleague's planner/session data (the row reattributes to the victim across the team calendar / Reports baseline, which the memory note flags assumes owner==assignee), and (b) orphan/lock their own record (post-reassign, their own ownsUserId() no longer matches, blocking further self-update/delete). The CREATE path already pins the incoming userId via `createOwnsUserId()` (line 121), and archived_tasks UPDATE already pins assignedUserId for its scoped branch (lines 286-287) — so the missing immutability pin on these three UPDATE rules is an inconsistency/oversight, not a documented allowance. No comment or helper anywhere sanctions userId reassignment; the only relevant design note asserts the opposite.

Severity ~medium is fair: requires a crafted client write (the exact threat model rules defend), no privilege escalation to manager, and READ being broad is separately intentional (visibility by design) — but the WRITE/tampering vector the design claims to close is genuinely open. Fix: add `request.resource.data.userId == resource.data.userId` to each of the three UPDATE rules.

CONFIRMED REAL. firestore.rules:270 — `allow update, delete: if isUserActive() && (ownsUserId() || isManagerOrAdmin())` for /work_hours. ownsUserId() (line 117-118) checks resource.data.userId == request.auth.uid, i.e. the EXISTING stored value; the rule never asserts request.resource.data.userId == resource.data.userId. Firestore evaluates resource.data as the pre-write doc, so a worker who owns a work_hours row passes the gate and can commit an update that overwrites userId with a colleague's uid. After the write the row is owned by the victim's uid: the original worker can no longer ownsUserId()-delete/fix it, and the team-wide AllUsersCalendar (broad isUserActive() read, maps every row by userId) renders the re-stamped row under the victim's name. The same unpinned-owner gap exists on /work_sessions update (238-242) and /break_sessions update (251-255).

NOT a deliberate design choice (Chesterton's fence checked and fails): (1) The rules file's own comment block (97-115) states the threat model is TAMPERING and the explicit intent that 'a worker can see a colleague's hours but cannot mutate them' — re-stamping ownership is exactly the tampering it claims to prevent. (2) The repo already establishes the correct mitigation pattern elsewhere: /archived_tasks update (line 286) pins request.resource.data.assignedUserId == resource.data.assignedUserId, deliberately making the owner field immutable on the scoped path; the absence of the equivalent pin on the three session/hours collections is inconsistent with the file's own practice — an oversight, not intent. (3) Both legitimate client update sites in WorkPlanner.jsx (lines 436-443 and 669-676) send only start/end/title/isWorkFromHome/isVacation/absenceType and never touch userId, so adding `request.resource.data.userId == resource.data.userId` would break no real flow.

Severity 'medium' is fair: requires an authenticated worker (no manager-privilege escalation), blast radius is integrity of planner/calendar/report data plus self-inflicted denial of cleanup, not confidentiality or account takeover. Relevant files: firestore.rules (lines 117-122 helpers; 238-242, 251-255, 270 rules; 286 precedent) and src/components/WorkPlanner.jsx (436-443, 669-676 legitimate updates).

CONFIRMED REAL after reading firestore.rules. The work_hours update/delete rule (line 270): `allow update, delete: if isUserActive() && (ownsUserId() || isManagerOrAdmin());`. `ownsUserId()` (lines 117-119) returns `resource.data.userId == request.auth.uid` — it tests the EXISTING stored owner (the pre-image). The rule never pins `request.resource.data.userId == resource.data.userId`, so the post-image owner field is unconstrained. Reproducibility check holds: a worker who owns a work_hours row can issue an update payload that rewrites `userId` to a colleague's uid; at evaluation the old userId still equals the caller so `ownsUserId()` passes and the write is authorized, after which the row is owned by the victim. The same gap is present on work_sessions (lines 238-242) and break_sessions (lines 251-255). I verified work_hours docs use auto-generated IDs (addDoc, WorkPlanner.jsx:430) with userId stored as a regular mutable payload field (set at create, line 432; not the doc ID), so the field is genuinely writable — and Firestore rules govern arbitrary SDK writes, not just the benign client edit path which omits userId.

Chesterton's-fence check fails to excuse it: the documented deliberate decisions here are the broad-READ/scoped-WRITE asymmetry and the manager/admin escape (lines 97-115) — both reason about WHO may mutate a row, not about whether the owner field itself may be reassigned. Decisively, the same authors DO apply the `request.resource.data.X == resource.data.X` pin idiom extensively on the users collection (role, teamManagerIds, scopedManager, seniorManagerIds, overseerIds; lines 160-176) precisely to stop ownership/scope re-assignment, proving they know both the risk and the idiom. They simply did not carry it to the owner field on the session/hours collections — an omission, not an intentional allowance.

Severity medium is accurate: this is an integrity/tampering issue inside an already-authenticated team (orphan one's own rows so they can no longer be self-deleted, or pollute a colleague's planner/reports), not privilege escalation or data exfiltration — it cannot flip manager-only approval fields and grants no role, and a manager/admin can remediate. Fix: add `request.resource.data.userId == resource.data.userId` to the update rules on work_hours, work_sessions, and break_sessions (allowing the manager/admin escape to still reassign if intended).

confirmed.title: Reports tab bar missing aria-current and role='tablist'
confirmed.detail: The three-button tab strip (Darbo ataskaita / Patvirtinimas / Kalendoriaus pakeitimų istorija) renders plain <button> elements inside a <div>. There is no role='tablist' on the container, no role='tab' on each button, and no aria-current or aria-selected on the active tab. Screen readers cannot identify this as a tab widget and cannot communicate which tab is selected. The active state is communicated only by color (brand border-bottom), violating §7 'color is never the sole signal'.
reasons: Confirmed by reading src/components/Reports.jsx:1068-1092. The tab strip is a plain <div className="flex border-b border-line overflow-x-auto"> containing three bare <button>s. The container has no role="tablist", the buttons have no role="tab", and the active tab carries no aria-current/aria-selected. Active state is signaled purely via className color/border (border-brand text-brand vs border-transparent text-ink-muted) — color is the sole programmatically/visually-distinct signal of selection, which violates DESIGN_SYSTEM §7 "Color is never the sole signal" (line 174) and the WCAG 2.1 AA gate. This is NOT a Chesterton's fence: the rest of the app consistently exposes the active-state semantic — AppHeader.jsx:52, SideRail.jsx:78, and BottomNavigation.jsx (lines 93/119/174) all set aria-current={active ? 'page' : undefined}, and UserProfileModal.jsx:93-115 implements a full role="tablist"/aria-selected pattern. The Reports strip is the lone outlier dropping the convention, so it reads as an oversight, not an intentional choice. The minimal house-consistent fix is aria-current="page" on the active button (matching AppHeader/SideRail/BottomNav); the fuller fix is the tablist/tab/aria-selected triad as in UserProfileModal. Caveat for accuracy: the claim slightly overstates the harm — each button always renders its visible text label, so a screen-reader user can still read and activate all three tabs; the genuine defect is narrower (the *which-tab-is-active* state is not exposed to assistive tech and is conveyed by color alone). The finding is nonetheless real: a medium-severity ux-a11y inconsistency and a §7 violation.

Verified against src/components/Reports.jsx:1067-1092. The manager-only tab strip is a bare `<div className="flex border-b border-line overflow-x-auto">` containing three plain `<button>` elements (Darbo ataskaita / Patvirtinimas / Kalendoriaus pakeitimų istorija). Confirmed: NO role="tablist" on the container, NO role="tab"/aria-selected/aria-current on any button. Each button carries only onClick + className; active state is conveyed solely by class swaps (active: `border-brand text-brand`; inactive: `border-transparent text-ink-muted`) — both purely color/border cues, with no programmatic selected state and no text/icon marker. So a screen reader announces three unrelated buttons and cannot tell which is active. Chesterton's-fence check REINFORCES the finding rather than excusing it: the same repo implements the full ARIA tab pattern correctly in sibling widgets — ManagerView.jsx:141-194 (role="tablist"/role="tab"/aria-selected/role="tabpanel"/aria-controls + focus-visible ring) and UserProfileModal.jsx:93-115 — so this is an inconsistency, not a deliberate project-wide design choice. DESIGN_SYSTEM.md mandates WCAG 2.1 AA (§7) and 'color is never the sole signal' (line 174/365), which this strip violates. Minor wording nit in the claim: the active tab also changes text color (not only the border-bottom), but both cues remain color-based, so the substance holds. Medium severity is appropriate (oversight-only view, keyboard focus still works, buttons are still operable; the defect is missing semantics + color-only selection indication, not total inoperability).

Confirmed by reading src/components/Reports.jsx:1067-1093. The three-button tab strip (Darbo ataskaita / Patvirtinimas / Kalendoriaus pakeitimų istorija) is a genuine tab widget: the buttons swap `activeTab` between 'report'/'approval'/'calendar-history' panels rendered below. The container is a plain `<div className="flex border-b border-line overflow-x-auto">` with NO role='tablist' and no aria-label; each button is a bare `<button onClick>` with NO role='tab', NO aria-selected, and NO aria-current. The active tab is distinguished ONLY by `border-brand text-brand` vs `border-transparent text-ink-muted` (color/border) — both states share `font-medium` and neither carries an icon, so there is no non-color or programmatic indication of selection. A screen reader cannot identify this as a tablist nor announce which tab is active. This is not a deliberate design choice (no comment/ADR justifies it) and it contradicts the repo's OWN established convention: the other two tab strips — ManagerView.jsx:141-177 and UserProfileModal.jsx:91-127 — both correctly use role='tablist'+role='tab'+aria-selected (ManagerView even adds role='tabpanel'/aria-controls/aria-labelledby). DESIGN_SYSTEM.md §6 makes WCAG 2.1 AA a mandatory gate and §4-A/§5 state 'color is never the sole signal'. The Reports strip is the lone outlier violating both. (Minor caveat: the claim attributes the 'sole signal' rule to §7; in this repo that rule lives under §4-A/§5 of DESIGN_SYSTEM.md — section number is slightly off but the rule and the violation are real.) The accessibility defect described is accurate and real.

confirmed.title: CombinedHoursSummary uses raw bg-blue-300 for a progress bar
confirmed.detail: The 'Planuota' (planned hours) progress bar fill uses `bg-blue-300`, a raw Tailwind color with no backing token. This is unrelated to any session state (it is a per-user hours chart), so it should use a neutral or brand token (e.g. `bg-brand/40` or a dedicated chart token) rather than a hardcoded palette value.

confirmed.title: request_notifications CREATE has no rate-limit enforcement and any active user can ring any manager's device
confirmed.detail: The create rule requires a non-empty `recipientId`, provenance stamping, and an unread flag, but places no constraint on the `recipientId` value itself — any active user can target any other user as recipient, including managers they have no relationship with. Each successful write triggers a Cloud Function that delivers an OS push notification to the recipient's registered devices. Firestore security rules cannot enforce rate limits, and there is no server-side throttle (the `notify.js` comment at line 12 explicitly acknowledges this). A disgruntled worker can spam any manager's phone with arbitrary notifications (within whatever FCM/Cloud Function quota applies). The `commentText` is capped at 2000 chars (line 371) so the lockscreen payload itself is bounded, but the frequency is not.
reasons: VERIFIED REAL against source. firestore.rules:363-371 (create) constrains only: active caller, non-empty string recipientId, provenance (caller's uid must equal createdBy OR userId), isRead==false, and commentText<=2000 chars. Crucially it places NO constraint binding recipientId to any relationship with the caller — any active user can set recipientId to any uid, including managers they have no relationship with. The provenance check only blocks forging the SENDER identity (you must stamp your own uid), not restricting the TARGET. Delivery chain confirmed: every create fires notifyOnRequestNotification (functions/index.js:159) -> sendToUser (line 77) -> sendEachForMulticast (line 88), pushing an OS notification to the recipient's FCM tokens; sendToUser has NO throttle (only gates: recipient-disabled-notifications, has-tokens). Firestore rules genuinely cannot count over time and there is no server-side throttle, so an authenticated active user can spam any manager's device. This is NOT a deliberate design choice masking a benign feature — the rule's own comment (lines 351-352) explicitly states it 'cannot rate-limit ... that needs a sender-side throttle', documenting it as a KNOWN residual gap, so Chesterton's-fence clears. Two minor inaccuracies in the claim do not defeat it: (1) the acknowledgment of the missing throttle is in firestore.rules:351-352, NOT 'notify.js line 12' as the claim states (notify.js:12 is about category tagging); the evidence exists, just at a different location. (2) commentText is further sliced to 100 chars in copyForRequestNotification (functions/index.js:132), so the payload is even more bounded than claimed — this strengthens, not weakens, the 'frequency not bounded' point. Severity [low] is fair: requires an authenticated active account, payload bounded, impact is nuisance/quota-exhaustion not data exposure.

Verified against source. firestore.rules:361-371 governs `request_notifications` create: it requires `recipientId` be a non-empty string, binds provenance (caller's uid must equal `createdBy` OR `userId`), requires `isRead==false`, and clamps `commentText` to <=2000 chars — but places NO constraint on the *value* of recipientId and NO relationship/team check between caller and recipient. functions/index.js:159 (`notifyOnRequestNotification`, an onDocumentCreated trigger) fires on every such create and calls `sendToUser` (line 77), which fans an OS push out to all of the recipient's registered FCM tokens; its only guards are the recipient's opt-out (`notificationsEnabledFor`) and token existence — no per-sender throttle. The intended design (bidirectional bell feed) is for legitimate notifications, not arbitrary cross-party spam; the rate-limit gap is a known-but-unmitigated weakness, not an intentional feature. The claim correctly acknowledges and works around the deliberate parts (provenance/clamp) and targets the genuine residual gap. Note: the cited \"notify.js comment at line 12\" is slightly off — notify.js:12 is about the type→category map; the rate-limit acknowledgment actually lives in the firestore.rules comment (lines 351-352). This misattribution does not change the underlying finding, which is real.

rejected.title: calculateCurrentTotalMinutes: timeAdjustments summed on top of manualMinutes that may already embed them
rejected.detail: Lines 147-150 unconditionally add every `task.timeAdjustments[n].durationMinutes` to the total. `timeAdjustments` is described as a legacy delta-correction array (read-only, no longer writable — see TaskDetailsModals.jsx line 361-363). When legacy manual-adjustment work_sessions that carry `isManualAdjustment: true` were written, they were ALSO baked into the task's `manualMinutes` field (line 138 sums `manualMinutes`). If a task has both `manualMinutes` that already incorporates a past adjustment AND a non-empty `timeAdjustments` array for that same delta, `calculateCurrentTotalMinutes` counts the adjustment twice: once inside `manualMinutes` and once through the loop. This inflates the live task total shown in the UI and in `useTaskTimeMonitor`, potentially triggering the 70%/100% time-limit alarms prematurely. The code comment says the correction "adding/removing was retired" but does not guard against double-counting for old tasks that carry both fields.
reasons:
Not a real bug — the "unconditional addition" of timeAdjustments is an intentional, internally-consistent design, and the data state the claim requires (the same delta embedded in BOTH manualMinutes AND a timeAdjustments entry) was specifically engineered never to exist. Verified against git history (commit 1b517e5 "retire delta editors", which removed the original writers):

1. calculateCurrentTotalMinutes (src/utils/timeUtils.js:131-174) computes total = manualMinutes + timerMinutes + Σ timeAdjustments[].durationMinutes + running session. This is correct IFF manualMinutes and timeAdjustments are disjoint partitions of the total.

2. The two legacy (now-removed) writers both enforced exactly that invariant:
   - The "total override" editor was the ONLY path that wrote manualMinutes near adjustments. It did NOT append a timeAdjustments entry; it set newManualMinutes = max(0, newTotalMinutes − adjustmentsTotal), with an explicit comment: "calculateCurrentTotalMinutes calculates total = manualMinutes + timeAdjustments, we must subtract the existing timeAdjustments from newTotalMinutes to get the correct manualMinutes." So manualMinutes was kept complementary to (never overlapping) the timeAdjustments sum.
   - The "delta-add" editor wrote timeAdjustments: [...old, newAdj] and did NOT touch manualMinutes at all. So appending an adjustment never folded into manualMinutes.
   There is no write path in the entire history where one delta lands in both fields. The claim's premise ("manualMinutes that already incorporates a past adjustment AND a non-empty timeAdjustments array for that same delta") describes an impossible data state.

3. The isManualAdjustment work_sessions (durationMinutes = delta to the REPORT total) are consumed only by report aggregators (DailyStatistics/Reports/MonthlyHours/CombinedHoursSummary via sanitizeReportMinutes) — a completely separate code path from calculateCurrentTotalMinutes. They never feed into manualMinutes, so no double-count there either.

4. TaskDetailsModals.jsx:361-363 confirms timeAdjustments is now read-only legacy history; no current code writes it (grep confirms only reads in src). The kept legacy data still satisfies the disjoint-partition invariant the read-side relies on.

This is a Chesterton's fence: the read-side "Add explicit time adjustments" loop is the deliberate counterpart to the writer's subtraction logic. No premature 70%/100% alarm inflation results because manualMinutes and timeAdjustments never carry the same minutes.

The claim's core premise is factually false. It asserts that legacy time adjustments were "ALSO baked into the task's manualMinutes field," causing calculateCurrentTotalMinutes to count the same delta twice (once in manualMinutes line 138, once in the timeAdjustments loop lines 147-150). I verified the actual historical write path of the now-retired delta editor (commit 1b517e5^, the handleAddAdjustment handlers in both src/components/TaskTable.jsx and src/components/TaskHistory.jsx). Each adjustment did exactly two writes: (1) created a separate work_sessions doc with isManualAdjustment:true and the durationMinutes, and (2) appended the delta to the task's timeAdjustments[] array via updateDoc. Neither path ever touched task.manualMinutes. manualMinutes is set ONLY by the timer-stop, quick-work, and call flows (src/utils/taskActions.js:124, src/components/TaskTimerControls.jsx:223, src/utils/sessionActions.js:481/552) — never by the adjustment handler. Therefore in calculateCurrentTotalMinutes the manualMinutes term and the timeAdjustments deltas are disjoint contributors and each delta is counted exactly once; there is no double-count in the live task total, and no premature 70%/100% alarm from this. The genuine double-count risk in this system is sessions-vs-task at REPORT aggregation, which is deliberately handled elsewhere by excluding isManualAdjustment sessions (DailyStatistics.jsx:579, TaskHistory.jsx:268, plus allowLarge handling in Reports/MonthlyHours/CombinedHoursSummary). The unconditional add at lines 147-150 is an intentional, correct design (Chesterton's fence): those deltas live nowhere else in the task total. The unit test at src/utils/timeUtils.test.js:155-162 codifies exactly this expected sum (manualMinutes 10 + adjustments 5+3 = 18). Not a real bug.

FALSE POSITIVE. The claim alleges `calculateCurrentTotalMinutes` (src/utils/timeUtils.js:147-150) double-counts because `manualMinutes` may already embed the same delta that `timeAdjustments[n].durationMinutes` carries. Reading the source refutes this — the two fields are two halves of a deliberately maintained invariant: total = manualMinutes + timeAdjustments, where manualMinutes is the BASE with adjustments stripped out, not a figure that embeds them.

Evidence from the retired delta editor (the only writer that ever set both fields), in git show 1b517e5^:src/components/DailyStatistics.jsx lines 917-933: it computed `newManualMinutes = Math.max(0, newTotalMinutes - adjustmentsTotal)`, explicitly SUBTRACTING the sum of existing timeAdjustments before writing manualMinutes. The in-code comment states the exact reason: "Since calculateCurrentTotalMinutes calculates total = manualMinutes + timeAdjustments, we must subtract the existing timeAdjustments from newTotalMinutes to get the correct manualMinutes." So manualMinutes deliberately does NOT include the adjustment; adding timeAdjustments back at lines 147-150 reconstructs the intended total exactly — no double-count.

The claim's stated mechanism is also factually inverted. It says legacy `isManualAdjustment:true` work_sessions were "ALSO baked into manualMinutes." In reality the legacy editor wrote the isManualAdjustment work_session as a SEPARATE reporting-side record (consumed only by DailyStatistics, and explicitly excluded from task-total summation and from exports — see TaskHistory.jsx:265-268 and DailyStatistics.jsx:558-565 dedup comments), while adjusting manualMinutes by subtraction. It never appended to the task.timeAdjustments array at the same time.

The timeAdjustments array is an even older artifact: no path in current or historical source appends to it (no arrayUnion, and the only `timeAdjustments:` write at TaskHistory.jsx:302 is a read-only reshape into an export object, not a Firestore write back to the task). With nothing ever writing both a manualMinutes increment and a matching timeAdjustments entry for the same delta, the double-count the claim describes cannot arise.

This is a Chesterton's fence with an explicit, documented reason (the retire-delta-editors commit notes "the double-count reconciliation is unchanged"). Lines 147-150 are the read-side half of that invariant, not an unguarded bug. The 70%/100% time-limit alarm inflation scenario therefore does not occur.

rejected.title: Reports.jsx spanDays uses UTC midnight parse for YYYY-MM-DD strings; DST boundary can produce 0-day span
rejected.detail: `new Date(endStr)` and `new Date(startStr)` parse bare YYYY-MM-DD strings as UTC midnight (per the ECMAScript spec). The delta divided by 86400000 is therefore purely UTC-day arithmetic, which is DST-independent — this is actually correct for the purpose of counting calendar days. However, when `startStr === endStr` (a single-day range), the difference is 0 ms and `spanDays` becomes `Math.max(1, Math.round(0) + 1) = 1`, which is correct. The only edge case is across a DST boundary where UTC midnight does not align with Vilnius midnight, but since both operands are parsed as UTC the subtraction is still exact. This is a low-risk item but worth noting that `Math.round` rather than `Math.floor` is used: a fractional day difference caused by leap-second insertion could theoretically round up and inflate `spanWeeks` by 1/86400, producing a fractional-minute over-attribution of baseline planned hours — negligible in practice.
reasons: Verified against the real source at src/components/Reports.jsx:352-355. The claim is self-refuting and the headline ("DST boundary can produce 0-day span") is false. `new Date(startStr)`/`new Date(endStr)` parse bare YYYY-MM-DD as UTC midnight per the ECMAScript spec, so BOTH operands sit in the same UTC reference frame. The subtraction (endMs - startMs) is therefore exact UTC-day arithmetic and DST-immune — a DST transition shifts wall-clock time, not the UTC instant of midnight-UTC for a date string. The delta between two distinct date strings is always an exact integer multiple of 86,400,000 ms, so Math.round can never collapse it to 0; a single-day range (startStr===endStr) gives delta 0 -> Math.max(1, 0+1) = 1, correct. The leap-second concern is moot: JS Date uses POSIX time (86,400 s/day, no leap seconds modeled), so no fractional-day inflation of spanWeeks is possible. The Math.round(...)+1 pattern is a sound, defensive choice that absorbs any sub-second jitter — a deliberate design, not a bug. The claim's own body concedes the code is "actually correct" at every step and labels the residual as "negligible." No real correctness defect.

Read src/components/Reports.jsx:352-356. spanDays = Math.max(1, Math.round((new Date(endStr).getTime() - new Date(startStr).getTime())/86400000) + 1). startStr/endStr are YYYY-MM-DD strings, which JS parses as UTC midnight per the ECMAScript spec. Because BOTH operands carry the identical UTC offset, the subtraction is exact whole-UTC-day arithmetic and is genuinely DST-independent — there is no Vilnius-vs-UTC misalignment in the delta since both endpoints share the same parse. The division always yields an exact integer for date-only inputs, so Math.round vs Math.floor is immaterial. Single-day case (startStr===endStr): delta is 0 ms -> Math.max(1, Math.round(0)+1) = 1, correct. The claim's own narrative concludes the code is correct ("this is actually correct for the purpose of counting calendar days", single-day "is correct"), and the leftover leap-second concern is itself spurious — JS Date/getTime() is Unix time and does not model leap seconds at all, so no fractional-day rounding can occur. No real defect; this is a correct, intentional UTC-day count, and the claim is a self-debunking note rather than an actual issue.

Self-refuting claim. Verified against C:\Users\karol\Desktop\WORKZ\.claude\worktrees\stoic-proskuriakova-aad0bf\src\components\Reports.jsx:352-355. The headline ("DST boundary can produce 0-day span") is contradicted by the claim's own body, which correctly concludes the arithmetic is DST-independent and exact. Both `new Date(endStr)` and `new Date(startStr)` parse bare YYYY-MM-DD as UTC midnight (ECMAScript spec). The difference between two UTC midnights N days apart is ALWAYS exactly N×86400000 ms — UTC has no DST, so no drift is possible and a 0-day span never arises from DST. Same-day range gives Math.max(1, round(0)+1)=1, correct. Because the delta is always an exact integer multiple of 86400000, Math.round and Math.floor are identical here (no fractional part), so the Math.round concern is moot. The leap-second inflation scenario is impossible: JS getTime() follows POSIX time and ignores leap seconds entirely. UTC-midnight parsing for counting calendar days is the correct, DST-immune approach and a deliberate design choice — Chesterton's fence intact. No reproducible defect exists.

rejected.title: endSession does not reset restoredSession.startTime when pausedSession itself has a pausedSession (nested interruption)
rejected.detail: When `endSession` restores a `pausedSession` (line 236), it sets `restoredSession.startTime = now.toISOString()` (line 241) so the post-interruption segment starts fresh. However, the restored session object still carries the ORIGINAL `session.pausedSession.pausedSession` — a second-level nested paused session from a scenario where the user had A → interrupted by B → interrupted by C. When C ends, B is restored with `startTime = now`. When B subsequently ends, `endSession` again reads `session.pausedSession` (now A's data) and resets A's startTime correctly. The issue is that if there is a chain deeper than 2 levels AND the user-document snapshot passed into `endSession` is stale (from the `userInfo` optional parameter), `session.startTime` for the restored session could be the OLD pre-interruption start (from the stale snapshot) rather than the `now` that was written to Firestore. The partial segment for the pre-interruption period was already logged; the resumed session would then produce a duration covering BOTH the pre- and post-interruption periods, double-counting the pre-interruption portion. This only triggers when `userInfo` is passed in as a stale snapshot AND there is a second nesting, which is rare but structurally possible.
reasons:
