The only critical write inside the try is `await updateDoc(userRef, updates)` at line 287 (logging at 314 and resume at 386 are already fire-and-forget), so a failure there is exactly the swallowed case that leaves activeSession still set on the user doc. Callers genuinely cannot distinguish failure from success: BreakTimer.jsx:82, QuickWorkTimer.jsx:245 (and the equivalent CallTimer path) each do an optimistic setOptimisticUserData(...) then `await endSession(...)` inside a try/catch that, on rejection, reverts the optimistic state and shows a Lithuanian error. Because endSession resolves even when the critical updateDoc fails, those catch blocks are dead for this error class — the optimistic 'ended/idle' UI is committed while Firestore still shows the session active. Notably useOrphanedSessionRecovery.js:65 attaches `.catch(...) -> logError`, i.e. it was written EXPECTING endSession to be able to reject; it cannot, so that path is also dead. Chesterton's-fence check: the intentional reason behind the catch was durable logging, not suppressing caller-visible failure, and the sibling startSession catch (lines 184-190) deliberately DOES rethrow — so this is an inconsistent asymmetry, not a considered decision to hide failures from callers. Severity is fairly medium: damage is bounded (clampSessionMinutes caps credited time) and self-heals on next load via orphan recovery, but the described intra-session window where UI shows idle while server shows active is real, and a subsequent startSession reading the stale activeSession into newPausedSession is a plausible consequence. The fix (rethrow after logError, matching startSession) is low-risk and makes the existing caller catch/orphan-recovery .catch paths functional.

rejected.title: Rule C — full-saturation red reused for task time-limit alarm (not quickWork)
rejected.detail: DESIGN_SYSTEM §4-C reserves full-saturation red exclusively for the quickWork session shell. TaskTimeLimitPopup renders its header as `bg-gradient-to-r from-red-600 to-red-700` and its acknowledge button as `bg-red-600 hover:bg-red-700` — both raw Tailwind red values (≈ #DC2626 / #B91C1C range) that visually match the quickWork shell (#EF4444). When a worker dismisses a time-limit alarm while the quickWork session shell is active, both are saturated red and the alarm cannot be distinguished from the background state by color alone. The fix is to use `feedback.danger` tokens (`bg-feedback-danger`, `hover:bg-feedback-danger-hover`) so the alarm reads as a system error color, not a session color.
reasons: Claim is not real as filed. (1) Factual premise is wrong: the popup does NOT render the quickWork shell color. quickWork shell = session.quickWork = red-500 (#EF4444); the popup header/button render red-600→red-700 (#DC2626/#B91C1C). The inline comment at TaskTimeLimitPopup.jsx:50-51 documents the deliberate choice to darken OFF red-500 ("red-500 was ~3.99:1") so the white title clears WCAG 1.4.3 — a Chesterton's fence proving the color was consciously chosen to differ from the quickWork shell. (2) Rule C (§4-C) reserves saturated red for the whole-screen session SHELL (named collision example: the offline banner), not modal chrome. A forced-acknowledge alarm renders through the canonical Modal over a 50%-black scrim that dims the entire background, with a white card body (Rule D), a distinct dark-red header band, a white XOctagon icon, and bold white title 'Laikas baigėsi'. The worker sees a dimmed screen + a distinct dialog, not two indistinguishable red fields. (3) The 'cannot be distinguished by color alone' correctness assertion is false — color is never the sole signal (icon + title + scrim + white card), exactly as Rules A/D require. (4) The proposed fix is a visual no-op: feedback.danger = red-600 (#DC2626) and feedback-danger-hover = red-700 (#B91C1C), the identical pixels bg-red-600/hover:bg-red-700 already produce (the canonical danger Button is itself defined as bg-feedback-danger/hover:bg-feedback-danger-hover = red-600/700). Swapping tokens changes nothing rendered, so it cannot fix a 'cannot be distinguished' problem. The only true grain is a minor token-hygiene nit (raw red-* vs the equivalent feedback-danger token), but that is not the filed claim, and it mirrors the deliberately paired sibling TaskTimeWarningPopup, which uses raw amber-700/orange-700 for the same bare alarm-chrome role — an intentional, established pattern for the two §6.3 'bare' popups.

Read src/components/TaskTimeLimitPopup.jsx (lines 51, 105), DESIGN_SYSTEM §4-C, and tokens.md. The high-severity "session-color collision" claim is not real as framed, for three reasons:

1. The colors don't actually match the reserved value. §4-C reserves saturated red for the quick-work SHELL, which tokens.md/tailwind.config.js define as session.quickWork.shell = #EF4444 (red-500). The popup uses from-red-600 to-red-700 (#DC2626 -> #B91C1C) for the header and bg-red-600 hover:bg-red-700 for the button. Neither is red-500. (red-700 #B91C1C does match the quickWork ACCENT, but §4-C governs the shell identity, not every red.)

2. The proposed fix is self-defeating. The claim says to switch to bg-feedback-danger, but feedback.danger DEFAULT = #DC2626 (red-600) — identical to the bg-red-600 the button already renders, and feedback.danger-hover is the same red-700. Swapping raw red for the token produces essentially the same pixels, so it cannot resolve a "cannot be distinguished by color" problem. That contradiction exposes the real nature of the issue: it is at most a token-hygiene nit, not a visual collision.

3. The "indistinguishable from the background state" mechanism is false (Chesterton's-fence / hidden-purpose check). This is a forced-acknowledge alarm rendered through the canonical Modal (bare, level="top", dismissible=false, role="alertdialog") over a 50% black scrim (feedback.scrim), per §4-D "content rides on cards, not bare color." The quickWork shell is darkened behind the scrim; the alarm is a centered white card. They are never adjacent same-saturation fields. Color is not the sole signal either: XOctagon icon + bold "Laikas baigėsi" title satisfy §4-A, and line 50's comment shows the author already darkened red-500->red-600 specifically to pass WCAG 1.4.3 contrast. The author is token-aware (line 80 body badge correctly uses bg-feedback-danger/10 / text-feedback-danger), so a transient danger-red alarm modal is a deliberate, semantically-correct design choice, not a §4-C violation. A minor raw-Tailwind-vs-token cleanup exists, but the claimed defect, its high severity, and its mechanism are wrong.

The collision premise is false on the actual token math, and the design is a documented, sanctioned choice. Source read: src/components/TaskTimeLimitPopup.jsx:51 (header `bg-gradient-to-r from-red-600 to-red-700`) and :105 (button `bg-red-600 hover:bg-red-700`).

Token facts (docs/design/tokens.md:77, 114, 307, 318): the quickWork SHELL — the thing §4-C actually reserves — is red-500 `#EF4444`. `feedback.danger` is red-600 `#DC2626`. The popup uses red-600 `#DC2626` and red-700 `#B91C1C`, both DARKER than the reserved shell value. The popup never renders `#EF4444` anywhere, so it does not reuse the reserved full-saturation shell red. The claim's assertion that the popup values "visually match the quickWork shell (#EF4444)" is wrong: #DC2626/#B91C1C are distinguishable darker shades.

The proposed fix is a no-op visually: `feedback.danger` IS `#DC2626` = red-600, which the acknowledge button already uses literally (`bg-red-600`). Swapping `bg-red-600`→`bg-feedback-danger` renders the identical pixel color, so it cannot resolve any color-collision; it is purely a token-rename.

Chesterton's fence confirms intent: the header comment (jsx:50-51) documents that red-500 was deliberately rejected and darkened to red-600/700 to clear WCAG 1.4.3 white-on-red contrast. DESIGN_SYSTEM.md §8 (line 222) explicitly names and endorses "the red `TaskTimeLimitPopup`" as a canonical pattern. §4-C (lines 94-97) scopes its reservation to the SHELL and enumerates exactly one offender to fix — the offline banner — not danger-feedback surfaces; feedback colors (success/warning/danger) are a separate, legitimate group (§tokens lines 143-144, 108-116).

What is genuinely (mildly) true is only a token-hygiene nit: raw `red-600`/`red-700` Tailwind values in a component technically violate Principle 4 ("tokens, not magic numbers") and could be expressed as `feedback-danger` tokens. But that is a low-severity style cleanup, not the filed claim. The filed claim — session-color/high, "alarm indistinguishable from the quickWork background by color alone, fix via feedback.danger" — is not real: the alarm uses darker reds than the shell, and the proposed token resolves to the same red-600 already present.

rejected.title: Firebase API key hardcoded in source and committed since initial commit
rejected.detail: The live Firebase API key (`AIzaSyDXaHCrL8hKgaEedSXEIT-XSxhmIcCEuXU`), authDomain, projectId, messagingSenderId, appId, and measurementId are hardcoded as `||` fallbacks in the Vite entry point. Git history confirms these values have been in the repo since the initial commit (`6d1d9fc`). The same values are also hardcoded verbatim (without any env-var fallback) in `public/firebase-messaging-sw.js` lines 19-24. Firebase client API keys are not server secrets and are intentionally public, BUT their presence in version history combined with no `VITE_FIREBASE_API_KEY` being required means: (1) the keys will always ship even if an operator wants to rotate them or use a staging project, (2) any future credential (e.g. a Cloud Function secret accidentally placed here) would follow the same pattern. The `.gitignore` correctly excludes `.env` files, but there is no `.env.example` or enforcement that the env vars are set before build.
reasons: Facts verified against source: src/firebase.js:7-13 does hardcode the Firebase client config as `||` fallbacks behind `import.meta.env.VITE_FIREBASE_*`, and public/firebase-messaging-sw.js:19-24 repeats the same values verbatim (apiKey AIzaSyDXaHCrL8hKgaEedSXEIT-XSxhmIcCEuXU, project darbo-planavimas). Git history confirms presence since the initial commit 6d1d9fc. However, this is NOT a real security issue — it is a deliberate, correct design choice. Firebase client API keys are public by design: they identify the project to Google's backend and are explicitly documented by Google as safe to embed in client code and commit to source control. Real access control is enforced by Firestore/Storage rules and Firebase Auth, not key secrecy, so committing them leaks nothing exploitable. The claim itself concedes this. Chesterton's-fence check passes decisively in the code's favor: (1) the env-var-first pattern means an operator CAN rotate keys or point at a staging project simply by setting VITE_FIREBASE_*, so the fallback does not block rotation — it provides a sane default; (2) firebase.js:16-20 adds an explicit console warning when env vars are missing, proving the fallback behaviour was intentionally designed and surfaced; (3) the SW header comment (lines 4-5) and firebase.js comments explicitly note these are the PUBLIC client config with no secrets, showing the team understands the secret-vs-public-config distinction. The claim's remaining arguments are weak: the "future credential might follow this pattern" point is speculative risk about code that does not exist, not a present defect; and the missing .env.example is a minor developer-experience nit, not a security vulnerability (the claim concedes .gitignore correctly excludes .env). No live secret is exposed and the override mechanism already exists, so this does not meet the bar for a real [security/medium] finding.

Verified against source at src/firebase.js:7-13 and public/firebase-messaging-sw.js:19-24, plus git history (initial commit 6d1d9fc) and .gitignore. The factual observations are accurate: the apiKey (AIzaSyDXaHCrL8hKgaEedSXEIT-XSxhmIcCEuXU), authDomain, projectId, messagingSenderId, appId, measurementId are hardcoded as `||` fallbacks, present since the initial commit, and repeated without env fallback in the messaging service worker; there is no .env.example. But this is NOT a real security issue. Firebase Web client config values are public identifiers by design, not secrets — they are shipped in every Firebase web app's client bundle and are visible to anyone via the browser network tab. Access control is enforced by Firestore/Storage security rules and Auth authorized-domains, not by keeping the apiKey secret. The claim itself concedes these are intentionally public. Chesterton's-fence check confirms deliberate intent rather than an accident: the `||` fallback is paired with an explicit code comment and a runtime console.warn (firebase.js:16-19) that loudly surfaces a misconfigured deploy, and the SW file comment explicitly states 'the PUBLIC client config ... no secrets'. The env-var override (VITE_FIREBASE_*) takes precedence, so an operator CAN point at a staging project or rotate config — directly refuting the claim's argument (1). Argument (2) (a future secret might follow this pattern) is hypothetical, not a present defect, and the missing .env.example is at most a docs/DX nit, not a security-medium. This is a false positive: real pattern, but misapplies secret-handling rules to non-secret public identifiers and ignores the commented, intentional design.

Facts all verified: src/firebase.js:7-13 hardcodes the Firebase config as `||` fallbacks (apiKey AIzaSyDXaHCrL8hKgaEedSXEIT-XSxhmIcCEuXU, authDomain, projectId, senderId, appId, measurementId); `git show 6d1d9fc:src/firebase.js` confirms the key is present at the initial commit; public/firebase-messaging-sw.js:19-24 repeats the same values with no env fallback; .gitignore excludes .env* and no .env.example exists. But this is NOT a real security issue. Firebase Web client config is non-secret by design — these values identify the project, they do not grant access; security is enforced by Firestore/Storage rules and App Check, not config secrecy. Every visitor to the deployed PWA already receives these exact values in the JS bundle and the registered SW, so committing them adds zero exposure. The report itself concedes this. Its two "consequences" are operability/hypothetical concerns, not reproducible vulnerabilities, and are further undercut by the code: env-var overrides (import.meta.env.VITE_FIREBASE_*) already let an operator rotate or point at staging without editing source, and there's a deliberate console.warn (lines 16-20) plus a SW comment "the PUBLIC client config ... no secrets" (lines 4-5) proving this is an intentional, documented design choice (Chesterton's fence intact), not an oversight. rejected.title: error_logs CREATE allowed for any authenticated user, not just active users
rejected.detail: `allow create: if isAuthenticated()` — the `error_logs` create rule accepts writes from any user who holds a valid Firebase Auth token, including accounts that have been disabled (`isDisabled: true`). A disabled user's token remains valid for up to ~1 hour after disable. A determined blocked user can therefore keep flooding the `error_logs` collection with arbitrary documents (up to their quota share) during that window. The logged record includes the caller's uid and userAgent verbatim with only a 2000-char message cap. This is a low-severity DoS/spam vector: the content is admin-visible only, but it costs quota. The rest of the writable collections correctly require `isUserActive()` for creates.
reasons:
The factual description is accurate: firestore.rules:406 reads `allow create: if isAuthenticated();`, and isAuthenticated() (lines 7-9) only checks `request.auth != null` — it does NOT verify the user doc exists or that `isDisabled != true`, unlike isUserActive() (lines 26-30) used by every other writable collection (e.g. line 381). A disabled Firebase Auth user's existing ID token does remain valid until expiry (~1 hour) — disabling blocks refresh, not the live token — so during that window such a user could append documents to error_logs. The record (src/utils/errorLog.js:91-101) includes the caller uid and userAgent verbatim (message capped 2000, stack 8000, componentStack 8000).

However, this is a DELIBERATE, in-file-documented design choice and the Chesterton's fence is intact. The rule carries an explicit rationale (lines 401-404): "Any authenticated user may APPEND a crash report (kept permissive so a broken session can still record its failure)." Gating the crash log on isUserActive() would require a get() on the user doc — but the logger exists precisely for the scenario where Firestore reads / app state are already broken, and a failing user-doc read would then suppress the very reports most worth capturing. So the looser gate is an intentional trade-off: maximize crash-capture reliability over write-tightness. The residual exposure is negligible: the client dedupes (5s window) and caps payloads, data is admin-read-only (no exfiltration, no privilege escalation), updates are immutable (allow update: if false) and deletes admin-only, the disabled-token window is bounded to ~1 hour, and this is a small-team internal tool where quota-spam is a marginal nuisance, not a real DoS. The proposed "fix" would actively undermine the rule's stated purpose. Not a real, actionable issue.

Verified against firestore.rules:401-410. The claim is mechanically accurate — error_logs create uses `allow create: if isAuthenticated()` (line 406), which only checks `request.auth != null` (lines 7-9), so a disabled user (isDisabled:true) with a still-valid token (~1h) would pass create but fail `isUserActive()` (lines 26-30). However, this is a DELIBERATE, DOCUMENTED design choice, not a defect (Chesterton's fence holds). The comment directly above the rule (lines 401-404) states the intent explicitly: "Any authenticated user may APPEND a crash report (kept permissive so a broken session can still record its failure); only managers/admins may read them. Entries are immutable; only admins may clear them." The author clearly understood the isAuthenticated vs isUserActive distinction — they correctly use isUserActive() everywhere else, including the adjacent task_templates block (line 381). The looser predicate is chosen on purpose because crash/error logging must survive exactly the broken/disabled/corrupt account states that break normal access (cf. the legacy missing-isDisabled bug documented at lines 15-25, where isUserActive-style gating silently denied reads). Requiring isUserActive() would itself do a get() on the user doc and would make the logger fail in the precise scenarios it exists to capture. The residual spam vector is heavily mitigated by design: writes are immutable (update:false), admin-only readable (read:isManagerOrAdmin), admin-only deletable (delete:isAdmin), capped at 2000 chars, and the ~1h token window is identical to every other write a disabled user could make. This is an accepted, intentional trade-off, not a real issue to fix.

The factual description is accurate: firestore.rules:406 reads `allow create: if isAuthenticated();`, and `isAuthenticated()` (lines 7-9) only checks `request.auth != null`, whereas `isUserActive()` (lines 26-30) additionally requires the user doc to exist and `isDisabled != true`. So a freshly-disabled account whose Firebase ID token is still valid (~1h, since Security Rules don't check revocation by default) can indeed create error_logs documents. Reads are manager/admin-only (407), updates are forbidden (408, immutable), deletes are admin-only (409), and message length is capped — so the blast radius is contained.

But this is a deliberate, documented design choice (Chesterton's fence), not a defect. The comment at lines 401-404 states the intent explicitly: "Any authenticated user may APPEND a crash report (kept permissive so a broken session can still record its failure)." A durable crash log must survive exactly the conditions that would make a stricter gate fail — `isUserActive()` performs a `get()` on the user doc, so a crash caused by that doc being unreadable/partial could not be logged at all if create required `isUserActive()`, defeating the log's purpose.

The DoS/spam framing is also the weakest part of the claim: any *active* authenticated user can already flood error_logs through the identical rule with no extra barrier. The disabled-vs-active distinction the claim hinges on adds essentially nothing — disabling a user was never a defense this rule removes, and the residual ~1h window for an already-disabled account is negligible (self-acknowledged "low-severity"). This is an intentional, justified permissive rule, not a real issue to fix.

rejected.title: Orphan rule: shift_logs — locked but never accessed
rejected.detail: match /shift_logs/{logId} { allow read, write: if false; } exists in the rules but no client code ever reads or writes the 'shift_logs' collection. The rule is dead weight that also signals historical intent that may confuse future contributors. Low risk, but worth removing to shrink the rule surface.
reasons: Verified against firestore.rules:222-225. The claim's facts are accurate: the block `match /shift_logs/{logId} { allow read, write: if false; }` exists, prefixed by the comment "SHIFT LOGS (unused collection — locked)", and a repo-wide grep confirms no client code under src/ ever reads or writes 'shift_logs' (all other matches are docs/audit/tooling files). However, through the correctness lens this is NOT a real issue: the rule is functionally correct (it denies all access, exactly as intended) and it is a deliberate design choice, not an accident. The explicit "if false" lock on an unused collection is a recognized Firestore hardening idiom, and the accompanying comment ("unused collection — locked") plus its documentation in DEPLOY_FIRESTORE_RULES.md prove intent — the Chesterton's-fence reason is right there: the placeholder signals the name is reserved/retired and guards against a future contributor wrongly assuming the collection is unprotected. The claim itself self-classifies as a low-risk "dead weight / shrink rule surface" cleanup, not a defect — there is no incorrect behavior, no security hole, and no malfunction. That is a stylistic surface-reduction suggestion against an intentional defensive rule, so it does not qualify as a real correctness issue.

Factual basis is correct — firestore.rules:223-225 locks `match /shift_logs/{logId} { allow read, write: if false; }` and no client code reads/writes `shift_logs` (grep finds only the rule itself plus audit/deploy docs). But the "remove it, it's dead weight" conclusion is wrong: this is a deliberate, documented design choice, not an accidental orphan. DEPLOY_FIRESTORE_RULES.md:31-32 explicitly states "Unused collections (`shift_logs`, `daily_stats`, `deleted_tasks`) are locked (`if false`) — the client never touches them, so they should not be writable." It is one of three sibling collections with the identical lock + identical "(unused collection — locked)" comment banner (firestore.rules:224, 260, 305) — a consistent intentional pattern. Under Firestore's default-deny model the block is behaviorally redundant, which is precisely the point: it is affirmative documentation-as-code marking the collection as known and intentionally sealed, so future contributors don't assume it was forgotten — the opposite of the claim that it "confuses" them. Prior audits (full-sweep-2026-06-21 and 2026-06-23 firebase notes) already reviewed this exact orphan and confirmed the `if false` locks are correct. Chesterton's fence: the reason for the fence is documented right next to it. Not a real issue.

Confirmed at firestore.rules:223-225 that `match /shift_logs/{logId} { allow read, write: if false; }` exists, and confirmed via repo-wide grep that NO client code (zero src/ references) reads or writes `shift_logs` — only the rule, audit docs, and deploy notes mention it. So the factual premise holds. But this is a deliberate, documented design choice, not accidental dead weight (Chesterton's fence is explicitly posted): (1) the rule has a self-documenting inline comment `// ---- SHIFT LOGS (unused collection — locked)`; (2) DEPLOY_FIRESTORE_RULES.md:31 explicitly documents that unused collections (shift_logs, daily_stats, deleted_tasks) are intentionally locked as a security posture — an explicit default-deny for collections that may hold orphan data but must never be client-accessible; (3) two prior full-sweep audits (2026-06-21 and 2026-06-23/06-firebase.md) already reviewed this exact rule and concluded it "correctly uses `if false`", i.e. it was examined and ruled correct, not flagged. The "confuses contributors" argument is refuted by the clarifying comment + deploy doc. This is the codebase's intentional belt-and-suspenders convention, not removable dead weight.

rejected.title: Orphan rule: daily_stats — locked but never accessed
rejected.detail: match /daily_stats/{statId} { allow read, write: if false; } exists in the rules but no client code accesses the 'daily_stats' collection. Like shift_logs, this is a dead rule left from a removed feature.
reasons: Verified against source. The rule block at firestore.rules:259-261 exists exactly as quoted (`match /daily_stats/{statId} { allow read, write: if false; }`), and grep confirms no client code accesses the collection — the only `daily_stats` mentions in src/components/DailyStatistics.jsx (lines 68, 180, 1479) are stale comments; the actual code hardcodes setDailyStats(null) and a no-op unsubscribe, with no collection(db,'daily_stats') query, read, or write anywhere. So the mechanical facts are correct. But this is NOT a correctness bug — it is a deliberate, documented design choice with Chesterton's fence posted: (1) the rule's own inline comment says `// DAILY STATS (unused collection — locked)`; (2) DEPLOY_FIRESTORE_RULES.md:31 explicitly documents these unused collections as intentionally locked; (3) two prior audits already examined and blessed it — full-sweep-2026-06-23/06-firebase.md:15 calls the `if false` locks 'correct', and the 2026-06-21 synthesis lists this locking as a deliberate hardening action. A default-deny `if false` rule on a never-accessed collection is the correct defensive posture in Firestore; it causes no malfunction, no coupling, no security gap, and removing it would weaken, not improve, the explicit deny. Flagging an intentional, audited, documented safeguard as a 'dead rule' mislabels working-as-intended as a defect.

Verified at firestore.rules:259-261: `match /daily_stats/{statId} { allow read, write: if false; }` exists, with the comment "DAILY STATS (unused collection — locked)". A targeted grep for any client accessor (`collection('daily_stats')`, `doc(...)`, `'daily_stats'` literal) across src/ returns ZERO matches. The only src references are non-load-bearing: a React state setter merely *named* setDailyStats that is hardcoded to setDailyStats(null) with a no-op unsubscribe (DailyStatistics.jsx:68,184-185), plus dead comments (lines 180-183, 1479) explicitly noting daily_stats is no longer relied on. The repo's own docs (DEPLOY_FIRESTORE_RULES.md:31, audits 06-firebase.md) already classify daily_stats as an intentionally-locked unused collection, same family as shift_logs. So the claim is factually correct: it is a genuine orphan rule for a collection no client touches. Chesterton's fence: the `if false` deny is a deliberate and CORRECT security posture (locking an unused collection prevents any access), so this is NOT a bug to "fix" by loosening or deleting — but the claim does not assert that. It is scoped as a low-severity firebase-coupling/dead-rule observation, which is accurate and not negated by any hidden purpose. Real as a minor housekeeping finding; the remediation is removal-after-confirmation, not a behavioral fix.

Verified at firestore.rules:259-261: `match /daily_stats/{statId} { allow read, write: if false; }` exists, preceded by the comment `// ---- DAILY STATS (unused collection — locked)`. Grep confirms the factual premise: no client code accesses the collection — the only `daily_stats` references in src/components/DailyStatistics.jsx are dead/legacy lines (a discarded `setDailyStats` setter and comments saying "Keep listening to daily_stats only if needed for legacy reasons"); there is no actual Firestore call to the collection. However, this is NOT a defect under the reproducibility lens — it is a deliberate, documented secure-by-default design choice. Locking an unused namespace with `if false` denies all access and prevents any future/rogue client from touching it; nothing can be reproduced as a problem. The intentionality is documented in at least three places: DEPLOY_FIRESTORE_RULES.md:31 ("Unused collections (shift_logs, daily_stats, deleted_tasks) are locked"), and audit reports docs/audits/full-sweep-2026-06-23/06-firebase.md:15 and full-sweep-2026-06-21, one of which states these "correctly use `if false`". Chesterton's fence is intact: the rule is intentional, signposted, and behaves as designed. At most it's a cosmetic/cleanup note (a few-line stale rule + dead comments could be removed), not a real issue.

rejected.title: Missing composite index: request_notifications — recipientId + isRead
rejected.detail: query(collection(db, 'request_notifications'), where('recipientId', '==', uid), where('isRead', '==', false)) — two equality filters on different fields require a composite index. No entry for 'request_notifications' exists in firestore.indexes.json. Firestore will return FAILED_PRECONDITION at runtime. The same compound query also appears in src/components/ManagerNotifications.jsx:80-83, which is the manager's live unread-count listener — it fires on every mount.
reasons:
The claim rests on a factually wrong premise about Firestore indexing. Two pure equality (==) filters on different fields do NOT require a composite index — Firestore serves them via its automatic single-field indexes (zigzag merge join). Composite indexes are only required when an equality/array-contains filter is combined with a range/inequality filter on a different field, or with an orderBy on a different field.

I read the real source: src/context/NotificationsContext.jsx:90-94 and src/components/ManagerNotifications.jsx:80-83. Both queries are exactly query(collection(db,'request_notifications'), where('recipientId','==',uid), where('isRead','==',false)) — two equality filters, and I grep-confirmed NO orderBy and no range filter is applied to either (results are consumed via snap.size / snap.forEach with no ordering). So no FAILED_PRECONDITION is thrown at runtime; the listeners work without a composite index.

This is also a deliberate, documented choice (Chesterton's fence). The firestore.indexes.json header comment and inline comments in DailyStatistics.jsx and TaskHistory.jsx explicitly state the correct rule: equality/array-contains alone is served by the automatic index and needs NO composite entry; composites are only added where an array-contains/equality is paired with a date/createdAt/status ordering or range. Every composite in the file follows that pattern. The absence of a 'request_notifications' entry is correct, not an omission.

The claim rests on a false premise about Firestore. I read both query sites: src/context/NotificationsContext.jsx:90-94 and src/components/ManagerNotifications.jsx:80-84. Both are `query(collection(db,'request_notifications'), where('recipientId','==',uid), where('isRead','==',false))` — two pure EQUALITY filters, no orderBy, no range/inequality, no array-contains.
